Description of Risk WP Risk Mitigation Measures  
Task 1.1: Digestate was found to be toxic for 
microalgae. Likelihood: low, severity: medium 
1 After identification of toxic compounds, strategies to 
reduce toxicity will be used (e.g. fed-batch feeding). 
Task 1.4: Slow rate of CO2 delivery into microalgae 
culture by the membrane carbonation system. 
1 Operating pH of microalgae culture to be optimised to 
drive the forward equilibrium of CO2 dissolution. Light 
intensity to be optimised to maximise photosynthesis. 
Task 2.1: Algae create a fouling layer of “kamikaze” 
cells that block any harmful light. Likelihood: high, 
severity: high 
2 Effective probe design utilising multiple spectroscopic 
methods. 
Task 2.3: Plating medium favours another class of 
bacteria compared to liquid medium. Likelihood: 
medium, severity: medium. 
2 16S rDNA will also be conducted on the liquid media 
and alternative plating media will be proposed. 
Task 3.1: Different species exhibiting varying degree 
of cell-wall weakening during autolytic incubation. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: low.  
3 Conditions for autolytic incubation will be made more 
extreme to induce cell-wall weakening (e.g. longer 
incubation time, N2 sparging) 
Task 3.2: Stirring forms emulsions resulting in 
significant solvent loss (<95% solvent recovery 
target). Likelihood: low, severity: medium 
3 Milder mixing technologies and other techniques to 
minimize solvent trapping (e.g. pH adjustment) will be 
simultaneously investigated and combined.   
Task 4.1: Low yield of suitable hydrocarbon chains 
from HEFA-SPK reactions with algal lipids. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: medium  
4 Multiple strategies in hydrotreatment steps, using 
separate hydrodeoxygenation, isomerisation, cracking 
catalysts, and catalysts will be pursued. 
Task 4.2:  Unsuccessful integration of HEFA-SPK 
with H2 production. Likelihood: low, severity: 
medium. 
4 Separate the two subprocesses to produce SPK, propane 
and H2 independently; this will still allow the new route 
to be more self-sufficient. 
Task 4.3:  Algae TCR oil leads to low-quality fuel. 
Likelihood: medium, severity: medium 
4 A more drastic set of temperature and pressure to be 
applied during hydrotreatment. 
Task 4.4: Purification steps are too complex and 
costly.  Likelihood: low, severity: high. 
4 Change the hydrotreatment steps in Task 4.1 to minimise 
purification requirements. 
Task 5.2:  Lack of accurate available data for each 
stage of life cycle, particularly from real world SAF 
manufacturing facilities (due to limited access to 
commercial-scale plant or IP restrictions). 
Likelihood: medium, severity: low 
5 We are currently partnered with the leading global SAF 
development company SkyNRG conducting a feasibility 
study for commercial scale SAF manufacturing in Ireland, 
providing direct access to accurate data in SAF 
manufacturing. 
Task 5.2: Project carrying out activities which are not 
in-line with the RSB Certification. Likelihood: 
medium, severity: low.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

GLOSSARY AI ELH ELT EOL EC EU FAIR GHG HMLS HSI IV ICP-MS Artificial Intelligence End-of-life hose End-of-life tyre End-of-life European Commission European Union Findable Accessible Interoperable Reusable Greenhouse gas High-Modulus Low-Shrinkage Hyperspectral imaging Intrinsic viscosity Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry LCA LCC LIBS Mt / kt / t NIRS PET POC REF SSP XRF r-PET v-PET 3T Life-Cycle Assessment Life-Cycle Cost Laser Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy Million tonnes / kilo tonnes / tonne Near Infrared Spectroscopy Polyethylene terephthalate Proof of concept Reference Solid state polycondensation X-ray Fluorescence Recycled Polyethylene terephthalate Virgin polyethylene terephthalate Tension, Time and Temperature EXECUTIVE SUMMARY We widely use PET in plastics and textiles, leading to over 20 Mt/y of complex waste worldwide for which no closed-loop recycling is viable today. Most complex waste is landfilled or incinerated. There is an urgent need for us to develop a circular solution to convert complex PET wastes from plastics and textiles back into high-added value products. WHITECYCLE unites 3 brand-owners, 1 PET converter, 2 waste managers, 1 digital deeptech for smart sorting, the world-leading enzymatic recycling SME, 1 LCA company, 3 UNIs/RTOs, 1 cluster, and 1 management firm. We aim to demonstrate two new processes combining strong scientific and industrial know-how: (i) innovative identification, sorting, and separation technologies that will dramatically increase the PET content of complex waste streams to 80%, and (ii) a disruptive enzymatic recycling process that is expected to yield pure PET monomers sustainably even for impure waste streams. We will repolymerise and recycle the PET monomers obtained. Thus, we will use 2 t of waste to demonstrate 3 highly technical PET-containing pilot series: 100 tyres, 1,500 m of lay-flat hoses, and 400 m² of multicomponent fabric that will be coated and used to manufacture 4 lines of technical garments. We will provide process design kits, LCA, and production cost estimates to PET manufacturers and waste management companies for rapid deployment and assure social acceptance. First projections show that our recycled PET will be competitive with virgin PET. We will conduct a full circle loop from real complex waste feedstock to representative product of the 3 use-cases at TRL 5. Then, a strong upscale study will allow the process steps to reach TRL 6 to 8. By 2030, we will enable the annual recycling of more than 2 Mt of PET, which corresponds to the amount of additional recycled PET needed to meet the EU’s 2030 targets. This will reduce emissions by around 2.06 Mt CO2eq and avoid the landfilling of more than 1.8 Mt of PET. CL6 -2021 -CIRCBIO -01 -04 CONFIDENTIAL WHITECYCLE PART B 3 1 Excellence 1.1 Objectives and ambition 1.1.1 General presentation of WHITECYCLE The European Commission’s (EC) Circular Economy Action Plan adopted in March 2020 has identified plastics as among the 7 key product value chains as a priority, given their environmental impacts and circularity potentials. Indeed, as the consumption of plastics is expected to double in the coming 20 years, the EC has taken targeted measures to address the sustainability challenges posed by this ubiquitous material and will continue to promote a concerted approach to tackle plastics pollution at a global level. To increase uptake of recycled plastics and contribute to the more sustainable use of plastics, the EC has proposed mandatory requirements for recycled content and waste reduction measures for key products such as packaging, construction materials, and vehicles, also considering the activities of the Circular Plastics Alliance. The European strategy is ambitious, envisioning the redefinition of the plastic value chain to make it 100% circular by 2050. It requires ambitious industrial actions coordinated at the European level. That’s why we aim at the industrial-scale implementation of 3 very different circular value chains by 2030, within 4 years after our project. We particularly address the use case of polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), the third most used polymer in the world with more than 82 Mt produced in 2019 worldwide and used in various applications (Figure 1). Figure 1: PET amounts in the two targeted applications i.e., plastics and textile in 2019 If PET recycling processes are commonly implemented for easy-to-collect and pure mono-material products (7 Mt of recycled PET (r-PET) produced in 2017 worldwide), the situation is very different for multi-material products, and massive volumes of PET waste currently end up landfilled or incinerated without any recycling possibility. Indeed, there are no available solutions at the industrial scale for the recycling or upcycling of these complex wastes due to current technical limitations related to the sorting, separation, and purification processes of the retrieved mono-materials. This complex mixed waste stream includes multilayer packaging, multicomponent textiles, and composite plastics (i.e., tyres, hoses, etc.). In a recent study, Singh et al. estimated 44 Mt of PET is used today in such complex compositions that it cannot be recycled with classical technologies. In our project, with conservative figures, our first estimation of eligible waste feedstock exceeds 20 Mt per year (1.5 Mt end-of-life tyres (ELT) and end-of-life hoses (ELH), 13.2 Mt multicomponent clothing, and 3.5 Mt multilayer packaging, 2 Mt of other goods and industrial applications). Such large amounts of waste generate soil and water contamination that can adversely affect soil and water biodiversity, significant greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, and many other negative impacts on human health. Rethinking the functioning of such a complex value chain requires efforts by all its key players (i.e., plastics and textiles producers, end-users, recyclers, retailers, consumers) to drive investment in the right direction. The objective of WHITECYCLE is to overcome the barrier posed by feedstock complexity via a set of versatile, disruptive, and fully scalable technological processes (mechanical, physico-chemical, and enzymatic) capable of infinitely retrieving and recycling PET, even from complex mixed waste to yield pure, industrially relevant monomer at any scale. We include a clear plan for the replication of the technologies to be implemented at the industrial scale (i.e., > TRL8) beyond our project, across a wide range of sectors implementing or manufacturing PET-containing products. Information recorded in detail and in standardized ways (using FAIR data principles) will enable interoperability among the components, transparency of the processes involved, and the reuse in similar contexts as well as new, innovative ones (data as an asset). The main outcomes of our project will be: • 3 industrially viable circular value chains from bin to highly technical products namely tyres, lay-flat hoses, and multicomponent textiles at TRL 6 or above for the different steps (recognition, sorting, enzymatic depolymerization, purification, monomer reuse). • Comprehensive LCA, LCC, social acceptability studies, and FAIR data for each of the 3 use-cases • Agreements for upscaling and implementation from 12 brand-owners, 3 PET manufacturers, and 3 waste managers/recycling equipment manufacturers 1 IHS Markit 2020 2 Singh et al., Techno-economic, life cycle, & socioeconomic impact analysis of enzymatic recycling of PET, Joule (2021), doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2021.06.015 PET WORLDWIDE 82 Mt, 2019 61% TEXTILESPLASTICS 39% 33 Mt IN CLOTHING 16 Mt IN OTHER APPLICATIONS carpets, non-woven textiles, geotextiles, etc. 23 Mt IN PACKAGING 10 Mt IN END-PRODUCTS 19.8 Mt 100% PET 13.2 Mt Multicomponent clothing 3.5 Mt multilayer packaging 6 Mt opaque bottles 13.5 Mt clear bottles 3 Mt tyres, hoses & rubber goods 7 Mt other goods - electronic, acoustic & insulation CL6 -2021 -CIRCBIO -01 -04 CONFIDENTIAL WHITECYCLE PART B 4 1.1.2 USE-CASE #1: PET IN TYRES AND REINFORCED RUBBER GOODS We must find solutions at the EU scale to produce sustainable tyres since natural resources are decreasing while waste is increasing. ELT are non-biodegradable wastes generated in large amounts all over the world. Worldwide, 1.6 billion new passenger car tyres are sold each year, representing 11 Mt of ELT. Tyres are the only parts of the car that touch the road. They must strike a balance between safety, traction, durability, energy efficiency, and manufacturing cost. Due to these competing needs, tyres are made of very complex composites that perform in harsh conditions. Figure 2 depicts the overall structure of a passenger car tyre. A tyre is made up of elastomers (natural & synthetic rubber), filler (carbon black & silica), reinforcement material (metals & PET textiles based on highly technical fibre), plasticizers, and other chemicals. Some fractions are currently very well retrieved, such as rubber and metal, but the embedded textile part composed of the carcass plies (thin textile PET fibre cords bonded into the rubber) and the cap plies made of PET, polyamide, or hybrid polyamide/aramid is not.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.2 KPIs and milestones: The table below summarises the key technological and go-to market (GTM) KPIs and milestones for our project, which are relevant to track progress along the pathway towards our objectives.

Table 6- Technological and GTM KPIs and Milestones  
Additional risk mitigation for scale-up and dual spectroscopic fusion/integration: The development and industrial integration of our VISUM LRS involves critical technical risks, particularly due to the spectroscopic fusion of LIBS + Raman modalities that differ significantly in their physical requirements. As discussed, challenges include:  
■ finding the case-dependent optimum laser-peak-power to excite the intense Raman signal while keeping the sample below its degradation threshold;  
■ designing an optimised collection optics to gather Raman scattered photons while keeping the mechanical compatibility with the sample geometry and the rest of our system’s components;  
■ addressing optical alignment and synchronisation issues among Raman excitation & its spectra acquisition and LIBS plasma generation & its spectra acquisition, and any possible sample displacement over the whole process;  
■ in the case of conveyed samples (as required for a sorting system), making the inspection system compatible with multipoint probing with a useful spatial resolution for industrial-grade challenges.  

These risks will be mitigated by:  
■ use of common hardware & software frameworks for both LIBS + Raman - albeit using different settings for each technology;  
■ developing 2 distinct implementation modes depending on the application: static spot - for single and non-conveyed samples (such as those in e.g. mining prospection) to be inspected in an at-line setup; and, dynamic spot - for moving samples, raw materials or products on a conveyor belt, like that of the waste recycling application (in this case if needed we will explore combining the dual spectroscopic fusion analyser with a traditional artificial-vision subsystem for properly targeting the inspection points in real-time).  
■ For high-speed throughput, we will use pre-trained lightweight ensemble models, parallel GPU processing & a hierarchical triggering system to optimise real-time analysis while maintaining detection accuracy.  
■ Staged validation approach (laboratory → pilot → industrial) will further de-risk performance challenges.  
■ From a resource standpoint, shared components and standardised subsystems will help manage complexity & cost.  
■ Industrial pilots with Deceuninck & OEM engagement (PICVISA) contribute to wide compatibility across sector-specific constraints.  

Among critical market risks, could be competitor innovation outpacing our project’s market readiness especially given the current acceleration in smart sensor deployment and advanced spectroscopy. To mitigate this, we will continuously monitor competitor activity and maintain a strong focus on product differentiation through superior performance, real-time AI integration, flexible deployment, service/support and pricing flexibility. Our optimal size is an advantage in terms of agility to react. Smaller players do not have the resources for reducing TTM and bigger players are too inertial for efficiently reacting to new market trends.

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the governance structure, decision-making processes, risk management, and quality assurance.
Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole Consortium description and complementarity: Our PhotonX consortium consists of 15 partners from 7 European countries (5 Member States: Spain, Germany, Ireland, France, Netherlands, and 2 Associated Countries: UK and Switzerland), combining excellent science and frontier research (ULM and HS), deep-tech SMEs (ALPES, NV, JLM, IRIS), large industrial (VW, CELSA) and SME (MyB) end-users (USAAR for fermentation systems), and expert organisations in standardisation (UNE), exploitation (TEMA), IPR (ICP), co-creation (BRITREST) and the leading industry association that promotes the sustainable development of organizations working in the field of photonics in Europe (EPIC). We cover the full value chain required to achieve our project’s objectives: development of advanced photonics technologies (Mid-IR, SERS, integrated photonics), complementary sensing modalities (electronic noses), multimodal integration and AI data fusion, validation in three high impact industrial use cases, and pathways to standardisation and market exploitation. Our consortium has been structured to integrate disciplinary and interdisciplinary expertise, linking sensing, engineering, data science, industrial process expertise, business exploitation, and social sciences/humanities perspectives in co-creation and upskilling (for digital adoption). Industrial/commercial involvement: Our industrial partners (MyBiotech, Van Werven, CELSA) act as early adopters, providing real-world complex industrial scenarios for validating the performance and reliability of the multimodal sensing platform, and for demonstrating its ability to respond to important market needs, anchoring our exploitation strategy. Technology SMEs in our consortium (IRIS, ALPES, NV, JLM) provide the pathway to scalable products and services, ensuring results are market ready. They are supported by UNE (standards), ICP (securing IP assets), BRITEST (alignment with industrial processes, operator requirements, and upskilling for adoption), TEMA framing the business case scenarios to generate market pull, and EPIC representing over 800 companies working in the field of photonics in Europe and fostering a vibrant photonics ecosystem by maintaining a strong network and acting as a catalyst and facilitator for technological and commercial advancement. This ensures our project balances scientific innovation with industrial viability and market exploitation. Gender and open science aspects: We are committed to gender balance and inclusiveness in project governance, stakeholder engagement, and industrial trials, and will apply a gendered innovation approach in requirements gathering, co-creation, and dissemination. Open science practices will be implemented through FAIR data management, open access to publications, and contributions to standardisation, always in tandem with robust IP Management for securing IP assets to ensure we protect European novelty for reinforcing competitive advantage. Strong project management, led by a leading SME in the photonics integration space: IRIS brings longstanding experience in managing complex EU Framework Programme, as well as commercial industry projects, to ensure efficient project management, governance, transparent communication, and risk management across our consortium. Its dual role as a technology provider and integrator positions it to align technical progress with industrial needs, keeping our project focused on delivery, exploitation, and impact. Role of each partner in the project and critical infrastructure: Table 16 provides a high-level summary of each partner organisation, along with a brief description of their role in PhotonX, along key infrastructure they leverage. Table 16- Core Expertise, Equipment/Critical Infrastructure and Role of each partner in the project At a total budget of just over €5.9M, this is a carefully co-designed plan that ensures value for money and supports delivery of our project objectives. Personnel costs are based on actual staff rates (researchers, engineers, developers) and realistic person-months, reflecting sector norms and regional cost differences. Equipment costs cover depreciation only, and travel budgets allow essential mobility for knowledge exchange, onsite testing, collaboration, complemented by extensive virtual engagement to ensure resilience and carbon responsibility. Only limited & justified subcontracting is required, as we possess the necessary expertise & infrastructure. Goods & services include lab consumables, prototyping materials, OA publishing, patenting, and dissemination activities (Table 3.1h). Other countries and international organisations: Not applicable. Participant No. * | Participant organisation name | Short name | Country | Type | Core role/expertise 1 Coordinator | IRIS Technology Solutions | IRIS | Spain | SME | Coordinator. Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy. Process Analytical Technologies. Multimodal builder/integrator with AI algorithms. Pathway to market. 2 | University of Ulm | ULM | Germany | UNI | Novel mid-infrared (MIR) spectroscopy techniques 3 | Alpes Lasers, S.A. | ALPES | Switzerland | SME | Compact QCL technology 4 | Hahn-Schickard | HS | Germany | RTO | Photonics integration, optoelectronic packaging, and sensor module design. 5 | NovoViz | NV | Switzerland | SME | Development an InGaAs SPAD camera module with on-chip processing 6 | JLM Innovation | JLM | Germany | SME | Chemical sensor arrays 7 | MyBiotech GmbH | MyB | Germany | SME | Use Case 1: Bioprocessing technology for pharmaceuticals production 8 | Saarland University | USAAR | Germany | UNI | Use Case 1: Bioanalytical platforms and fermentation systems 9 | Van Werven | VW | Netherlands | LE | Use Case 2: Plastics Recycling 10 | CELSA OPCO, S.A. | CELSA | Spain | LE | Use Case 3: Steelmaking 11 | Britest | BRITEST | UK | SME | Industry cocreation, chemical process expertise, upskilling 12 | TEMATYS | TEMA | France | SME | Business Scenario & Exploitation Strategy 13 | Incelom Commercial Pathways | ICP | Ireland | SME | Intellectual Property Management 14 | Asociación Española de Normalización | UNE | Spain | Other | Standardisation activities to facilitate market uptake 15 | European Photonics Industry Consortium | EPIC | France | Other | Communication and Dissemination and ecosystem clustering & networking O1. To scale up advanced photonics sensing platforms to TRL6: Mid-Infrared Spectroscopy with Interband & Quantum Cascade Laser (ICL/QCL) technology (O1a), Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (O1b), and InGaAs single-photon avalanche diodes (SPAD) camera module with on-chip processing (O1c) O1a. To develop mid‑infrared spectroscopy (based on FTIR (=> broadband spectra for wide analyte coverage) with the alternative option of QCL/ICL (=> narrowband spectra for target analyte coverage) coupled to iHWGs). Our objective is to design and optimise integrated hollow waveguides (iHWGs) that act as miniaturised gas cells and photon conduits. These iHWGs will form the core of a modular mid-infrared spectroscopy system in which they are coupled with appropriate sources and detectors. FTIR broadband sources will enable simultaneous detection and discovery of multiple analytes, while QCL/ICL narrowband emitters will provide targeted coverage for specific low-concentration compounds. By tailoring the geometry, optical path length, and fabrication materials of the iHWGs, compact, robust, and versatile sensing modules will be created that operate reliably even in harsh industrial environments. The development will follow an iterative optimisation strategy, ensuring that different iHWG structures are adapted to path-length requirements, environmental conditions, and use-case targets, thereby delivering a complete, application-oriented MIR sensing system. KPIs: Development of tailored iHWG structures for harsh environments (corrosive gases, high humidity, high temperatures). Demonstration of broadband FTIR-iHWG systems for multi-analyte detection and narrowband QCL/ICL-iHWG systems for single-analyte detection for low concentrated target molecule. Stable operation of both systems. Validation in PhotonX use cases. ■Significantly lower energy consumption (30% reduction), much faster acquisition and lower latency (20–100× improvement in response time), higher sensitivity (20–30% lower LoD), and a footprint reduced by more than half compared to current devices. ■Compact head for 4-5× volume reduction with improved uptime and reduced calibration needs. O1b. To demonstrate a fully integrated indium gallium arsenide (InGaAs) single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) sensor with on-chip photon count accumulation, enabling real-time data processing directly at the pixel level and reducing reliance on external hardware. This approach will deliver unprecedented system-level performance in terms of data throughput, frame rate, and compactness, paving the way for an efficient and fast short-wave infrared (SWIR) imaging in, e.g. industrial machine vision solutions. A second critical objective is to establish a new state-of-the-art in sensor format by successfully fabricating a 192×128 InGaAs SPAD array, the largest format available to date. KPIs: Sensor/Hardware: ■Array size: 192×128; ■Integration yield: >80% of hybridized sensor-readout circuit (ROIC) assemblies are functional; ■Frame rate: Demonstrate up to 8000 fps. System-Level: ■On-chip processing power: Reduce external data transfer by ≥60% compared to baseline readout; Innovation/Exploitation: Scalability: ■Roadmap to ≥150k pixels in future projects; ■IP creation: ≥ 1 patent filed. O1c. To develop and demonstrate a robust, scalable, and intelligent SERS sensing platform for real-time industrial monitoring, through a double approach, integrated with AI-driven analytics: The key results will be:

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Write the Management structure and procedures section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

3.2 Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole We bring together a world-class team to drive the transition to carbon-neutral steelmaking through system-level R&D that tackles material and process challenges while ensuring efficiency, sustainability, and high product quality. Our 14-partner consortium integrates complementary expertise across the research-industry value chain (refer to fig. 1): leading steelmakers (CELSA, ERAMET, AMMR), machinery (HAE), Industry 4.0 tech providers (IRIS), technical facilitators & process experts (BRITEST), as well as business experts (ICP) ensure market-oriented innovation and scale-up. Research excellence is provided by universities and institutes (REC, POLITO, CEA, TEC, SINTEF), who are committed to both Open Science and industrial exploitation. UNE contributes standards alignment, and WIP supports communication, dissemination, and IP-Open Science balance. We also demonstrate strong gender equity, with 11 partners having Gender Equality Plans and active participation of women across all roles. Together we bring the expertise needed to design, validate, and scale integrated solutions while ensuring lasting impact. We come from 6 EU Member States (Italy, France, Spain, Germany, Ireland) and 2 Associated Countries (UK, Norway). ERAMET has a global footprint (& also involves its French R&D unit, ERAMET IDEAS). Our Project Coordinator, Andrea Salimbeni, Head of Unit of “Raw materials & carbon recycling” REC, senior R&D engineer, is an expert in thermochemical processes for the valorisation of waste streams for biofuels and biomaterials production. He possesses the knowledge, experience, and skills to lead and ensure the seamless cooperation, Scientific and Technical decision-making process, and transfer of strategic information across teams, to ensure a high quality and innovative outcome. He is an excellent communicator, analytical thinker & problem solver and is effective in team mobilisation. He is the inventor of two patents and author of 12 peer-reviewed articles focused on our concept. Overall, REC, with its experienced research teams & excellent research infrastructure, will lead our consortium, tackling the S&T challenges, and uniting their expertise, patents, coordination of EU projects & cutting-edge facilities. The table below outlines each partner’s core expertise, role, and key infrastructure contributions. 

Table 11- Core Expertise, Equipment/Critical Infrastructure and Role of each partner in our project 

At a total budget of under €13.9 M, this is a carefully co-designed plan that ensures value for money and supports delivery of our project objectives. Personnel costs are based on actual staff rates (researchers, engineers, developers) and realistic person-months, reflecting sector norms and regional cost differences. Equipment costs cover depreciation only, and travel budgets allow essential mobility for knowledge exchange, onsite testing, collaboration, complemented by extensive virtual engagement to ensure resilience and carbon responsibility. Only limited subcontracting is required, as we possess the necessary expertise and infrastructure. Goods and services include lab consumables, prototyping materials, OA publishing, patenting, and dissemination activities (Table 3.1h). 

Other countries and international organisations: Not applicable

1. To develop the optimal NextSteel process for bioanthracite production bench scale (TRL4) 
Biowastes for bioanthracite production for metallurgy industry are selected from among sludge, urban waste and agrifood waste. We identify the optimal HT conditions for biowaste pretreatment to maximize the carbon recovery. Moreover, chemical leaching and pyrolysis in presence of additives are also tested in small pilot units (TRL4), so that to maximize the extraction of inorganic nutrients and obtain a low ash bioanthracite from the solid. From each selected biowaste, >200 g bioanthracite will be produced and we will perform work to maximize the carbon recovery rate as biogenic reducing agents (bioanthracite, gas). The work will, in combination with tests on liquid phase, culminate in the optimal process configuration being determined, and the selection of 4 biowastes (or mixtures) for the pilot scale tests by M24. 
KPIs: 
- HTyields > carbon yield into hydrochar 
- Carbon content of produced bioanthracite samples > % d.b; 
- Ash content of produced bioanthracite samples 20%; 
- Extraction of >9% phosphorous by leaching of hydrochar 

To develop and determine the optimal NextSteel process for green gas production bench scale (TRL4) 
We will test the optimal conditions to convert the biogenic organic carbon from the liquid by AD and SCWG. Particularly, the aqueous phase from HTT is processed to maximize the production of a green gaseous reducing agent. The AD tests will be performed in 500 ml glass stirred reactors by REC, while catalytic SCWG will be performed in continuous mode by CEA with an innovative catalyst. Validation of hydrogen production at pilot scale with new catalyst for SCWG and limitation of conversion temperature. We will determine the organic carbon conversion rate for both AD and SCWG tests. Moreover, gas composition will be determined to assess the applicability as reducing agent in multiple routes, including DRI, EAF and BF-BOF processes. 
KPIs 

To test the combined production of green hydrogen and carbon by methane cracking (TRL 4) 
The green gas produced processing the HTT liquid by means of anaerobic digestion and SCWG, after CO2 separation, consists of CH4 (From AD) and a mix CH4/H2 (from SCWG). As for fossil methane, catalytic pyrolysis represents a promising process to obtain pure hydrogen, usable to decarbonize the direct reduction of Iron. Our objective is to use bioanthracite and SiMn slag as catalyst in a 900°C methane pyrolysis reactor, to maximize the methane cracking and fully valorise the spent catalyst a C-enriched bioanthracite from both the steelmaking and SiMn production processes. Tests are performed by RE-CORD in a quartz furnace. 
KPI: 
- >70% methane conversion into hydrogen; 
- >5% C on dry basis deposited on SiMn slag after cracking 

To scale up the integrated process and to perform experiments in real environment (TRL4-6) 
We will construct a continuous fast-HTT prototype of > 250 kg/h capacity, and the process will be validated in a real environment for minimum 120 hours. The performance of SCWG for turning the HTT aqueous phase into green gas will be assessed in a pilot scale unit, upscaled to TRL6 by using new design catalytic reactor, coupling with salt separation and long test runs with real HTT aqueous phase. AD tests will also be performed in a 1 m3 anaerobic digester. Leaching trials (>20 tests) will be performed on produced hydrochar as an input
